topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy dropping onto a wooden desk. My fingers hovered over the screen, tracing the unfamiliar name printed at the top of the subject line. A slow, heavy sigh escaped my lips, a sound that seemed to carry the weight of weeks of quiet disappointment. I clicked. The article, my meticulously crafted piece on urban decay, was undeniably there, the prose mine, the arguments mine. But the byline, the proud assertion of authorship, belonged to a stranger. I reread the text, the familiar cadence suddenly feeling alien, a borrowed coat that didn't quite fit. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning in protest, and stared out the window at the indifferent cityscape. There was nothing left to fight; the ink was dry, the publication made. I simply closed the laptop, the click sounding final and utterly hollow.

[story 2]

I picked up the printed copy from the mail slot. The paper felt crisp, the typeface sharp. My gaze snagged on the name printed beneath the headline. A coldness seeped into my chest, settling low, like deep water. I didn't move immediately; I just held the paper, letting the silence stretch between me and the news. When I finally looked back at the text, the words swam slightly. It was my voice, my perspective, laid bare for the world, yet someone else had claimed the credit. A faint tremor ran through my hand as I folded the page, the movement slow, deliberate, lacking any real energy. I placed it on the shelf, not bothering to look for an immediate response or a rebuttal. The effort to salvage anything felt too exhausting.

[story 3]

The phone call ended, leaving a ringing silence in my ear. I stared at the notification popping up on my secondary monitor—a link to the published piece. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. I clicked through, and the reality unfolded: my work, polished and argued, attributed to an imposter. A strange numbness began to spread through my limbs. I stood up, walked to the window, and watched a distant car pass by, its movement smooth and uncaring. I didn't bother to check my drafts folder; the effort of comparison was simply too much. I walked back to my desk, picked up a pen, and instead of trying to correct the error, I simply began to organize the stack of unread correspondence, each paper a small, manageable weight.
